image by = barrierbreakFree WCAG Compliance Checkers: A Practical Guide
You don’t need an enterprise budget to start checking your site for accessibility. Some of the most useful free WCAG compliance checkers are a browser extension or a menu click away, and they’ll surface real problems in minutes. The catch — and it’s an important one — is knowing what these tools can and can’t tell you. Used well, they’re a genuine head start. Treated as a compliance guarantee, they’ll give you false confidence. Here’s an honest look at the best free options and how to get value from them.
What a Free WCAG Compliance Checker Actually Does
A WCAG compliance checker scans your web page and flags where it fails the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines — missing alt text, low color contrast, empty links and buttons, unlabeled form fields, and similar code-level issues.
The crucial thing to understand up front: automated checkers catch only a portion of accessibility issues — the objective ones a machine can verify. They can confirm an image has alt text; they can’t judge whether that alt text is meaningful. So think of these tools as a fast first pass, not a verdict.
The Best Free WCAG Compliance Checkers
Here are the free tools worth having in your kit, and what each is genuinely good at.
WAVE (WebAIM)
WAVE, from the nonprofit WebAIM, is one of the most popular free checkers, available as a browser extension and online tool. Its signature feature is visual, in-context feedback: it overlays icons directly on your page showing errors, alerts, and structural elements.

• Best for: beginners and visual learners; seeing issues in context.

• Strength: intuitive; great for understanding where problems live.
axe DevTools (Deque)
axe DevTools runs inside your browser’s developer tools and is built on axe-core, the open-source engine that powers much of the industry. It’s known for a low false-positive rate — the issues it reports are usually real.

• Best for: developers already working in DevTools.

• Strength: reliable, trusted engine; clear WCAG mapping.
Google Lighthouse
Lighthouse is built right into Chrome DevTools, so there’s nothing to install. Its accessibility audit (powered by axe-core) gives you a quick score alongside performance and SEO checks.

• Best for: a fast, zero-setup snapshot during development.

• Strength: already in your browser; good for quick, repeated checks.
Microsoft Accessibility Insights
Accessibility Insights from Microsoft offers automated checks plus guided manual assessments — walking you through the checks a machine can’t perform on its own.

• Best for: teams wanting structure around manual testing too.

• Strength: bridges automated and manual with guided workflows.
ANDI
ANDI is a free bookmarklet from the US Social Security Administration, widely used in government accessibility testing. It inspects elements in detail and shows how assistive technology will interpret them.

• Best for: deeper element-level inspection, including Section 508 contexts.

• Strength: detailed output; trusted in public-sector testing.
Consultancies often maintain their own free tools too. BarrierBreak’s rundown of free must-have web accessibility testing tools is a useful companion list, and includes their own browser extension, A11yInspect, which adds on-page highlighting and code-level guidance to the mix.
How to Actually Use These Tools Well
Owning the tools isn’t the same as testing effectively. A practical routine:

• Start with one automated checker, not five. Multiple overlapping scanners create confusion, not better coverage.

• Run it early and often — during development, not just before launch.

• Fix the confirmed issues first — missing alt text, contrast, labels, empty controls.

• Read the alerts, don’t just clear them. Many tools flag “needs review” items that require your judgment.

• Then test manually. Keyboard navigation and a screen reader will reveal what no automated checker can.
That last step is where real accessibility is won or lost.
What Free Checkers Can’t Do
Be clear-eyed about the limits:

• They can’t confirm meaning. Alt text, link text, and reading order need human judgment.

• They can’t complete a task. Only manual testing reveals whether someone can actually check out or sign up with a screen reader.

• They can’t guarantee compliance. A clean automated report means you’ve cleared the machine-checkable issues — not that your site is WCAG conformant.
For confidence beyond the automated layer, many organizations pair free tools with expert review; the accessibility specialists at BarrierBreak combine automated, manual, and assistive-technology testing precisely because no free checker tells the whole story.
Common Mistakes to Avoid

• Trusting the score. A green result is a floor, not a finish line.

• Running every tool at once. One good checker plus manual testing beats a pile of overlapping scanners.

• Ignoring “needs review” alerts. The manual-judgment items are often the ones that matter most.

• Skipping keyboard and screen reader testing. That’s where the majority of real barriers hide.
Conclusion
Free WCAG compliance checkers are genuinely valuable — WAVE, axe DevTools, Lighthouse, Accessibility Insights, and ANDI can all surface real issues in minutes without costing a cent. The key is using them for what they are: a fast, reliable first layer that catches the obvious, machine-verifiable problems. Pick one that suits your workflow, run it early and often, fix what it finds — and then do the manual and screen reader testing that no automated tool can replace. Do both, and these free tools become the front end of a genuinely accessible product, rather than a false sense of security.
FAQ
Can a free WCAG compliance checker make my site fully compliant? No. Free checkers catch only the machine-verifiable issues — a portion of the total. Full WCAG conformance requires manual testing, keyboard testing, and screen reader testing on top of any automated scan.
Which free WCAG checker is best for beginners? WAVE is a common favorite because it overlays feedback visually on your page, making it easy to see where issues are. Lighthouse is also beginner-friendly since it’s built into Chrome with nothing to install.
What’s the difference between WAVE, axe, and Lighthouse? WAVE emphasizes visual, in-context feedback. axe DevTools runs in developer tools with a trusted, low-false-positive engine. Lighthouse (which uses the same axe-core engine) gives a quick built-in score in Chrome. Many developers use axe or Lighthouse for speed and WAVE for clarity.
Do I need more than one checker? Usually not. Running several overlapping scanners tends to create confusion rather than better coverage. One solid automated tool plus proper manual testing is the stronger approach.
Are free checkers enough for legal compliance (ADA, Section 508)? They help, but they aren’t sufficient alone. Legal frameworks reference WCAG conformance, which requires manual verification. Automated tools reduce risk but don’t establish full compliance.
How often should I run a WCAG checker? As often as practical — ideally integrated into your development workflow so issues are caught as they’re introduced, not discovered right before launch.
